summary: Joe Reis argues that the current AI hype in corporate boardrooms follows the 'Underpants Gnomes' pattern of 'AI...?...Profit' without understanding the middle steps. He observes that executives pushing AI initiatives often don't understand data fundamentals, while those who do understand data recognize how difficult it is to get basics right. Most companies can barely do basic dashboards yet dream of AI overlords running their organizations.
key_points: CXO roles are immature, misunderstood, and often adversarial with short budgets and unrealistic timelines
Companies want AI strategies without any foundational data strategy
ML/AI projects typically fail because organizations skip building solid data foundations
The gap between AI aspirations and Excel-dependent reality in most organizations is vast
AI trained on human data cannot save us from human-created organizational problems
money_quotes: It's like the people who understand data know it's difficult and want to get the basics right, and the ones pushing AI are doing it for…other reasons.
Not having an 'AI Strategy' is akin to not having a website. 'WTF…where's the AI?' screams a CEO who might not even understand what the A and the I stand for.
The chasm between AI overlords running an organization and the Excel-hell in most organizations is very wide.

There's a notion that AI will compensate for human deficiencies and 'make the world a better place,' essentially saving us from ourselves. I'm very bullish on the potential of AI, but I disagree that our AI overlords will save us from ourselves.
Focus on your data strategy before your AI strategy. But these days, companies often want to jump straight into AI without any strategy.
key_insight: The executives most excited about AI are often the ones who least understand data, while those who understand data know that most organizations haven't mastered the basics needed to make AI initiatives successful.
